nativeRange.habitat: Floodplains, wetlands, marshes, swamps, stream banks, and ditches; grows at depths up to ~0.5 m

summary: Creeping Jenny (Lysimachia nummularia) is a low-growing freshwater stem plant with small, rounded coin-shaped leaves. Adaptable across a wide range of water conditions and beginner-friendly, it works well as a foreground carpet plant and is safe for shrimp tanks.
careNotes: Creeping Jenny is a versatile stem plant available in both a standard green form and a golden-yellow cultivar sold under the name 'Aurea'. In the aquarium it grows with a low, spreading habit and works well in the foreground, though stems can extend well beyond 50 cm if left unchecked. Regular trimming to around 10–15 cm above the substrate keeps the plant compact and encourages dense, bushy growth. Added CO2 is beneficial but not required for healthy growth. Quality aquarium soil and regular liquid fertilisation improve results noticeably, particularly for the golden form. The plant tends to be sensitive to sudden changes in water parameters and may melt back when first introduced to a new tank; acclimating it slowly reduces the risk of this. Yellowing or wilting foliage can indicate elevated nitrate levels, making Creeping Jenny a useful early-warning indicator of water quality in a planted tank. The plant is generally considered safe for shrimp and most invertebrates, and is unpalatable to the majority of fish, though large herbivorous species may graze on it. When grown partially or fully out of water — as in a paludarium — it can produce small yellow flowers. In larger aquariums it can also serve as midground filler rather than a strict foreground plant.
breedingNotes: Creeping Jenny is propagated most easily from stem cuttings. Trim a healthy stem segment containing at least two nodes, remove the lower leaves to expose the nodes, and press the cutting into the substrate. New roots typically establish within a week or two. The plant also spreads through stolons (horizontal runners) in emersed or paludarium settings, producing new plantlets naturally along the runner. Propagation from seed is possible but rarely attempted in cultivation, as seeds are uncommon.
